name: Une journée à la salle de sport

sentences.base_language: Antoine had been meaning to get back into shape for months and finally took out a gym membership.
sentences.target_language: Antoine voulait se remettre en forme depuis des mois et a finalement pris une inscription à la salle de sport.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Voulait depuis des mois** — **depuis** + imparfait to express an intention that had been ongoing up to a past moment: **voulait se remettre en forme depuis des mois** (had been meaning to get back into shape for months). Unlike English, which uses the past perfect progressive, French uses the imparfait + **depuis**: the imparfait frames the desire as a continuous background state stretching up to the moment of action. Compare: **il cherchait du travail depuis six mois** (he had been looking for work for six months).
**Se remettre en forme** — pronominal verb **se remettre** + **en forme** meaning to get back into shape, to get fit again. **Se remettre** (pronominal of **remettre**) means to recover, to resume, or to put oneself back: **se remettre d'une maladie** (to recover from an illness), **se remettre au travail** (to get back to work), **se remettre en forme** (to get back into shape). **En forme** is a fixed adverbial expression: **être en forme** (to be in good shape/form), **se sentir en forme** (to feel fit).

sentences.base_language: On his first morning, he arrived early and felt a mix of motivation and slight intimidation.
sentences.target_language: Le premier matin, il est arrivé tôt et a ressenti un mélange de motivation et d'une légère intimidation.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est arrivé** — **arriver** takes **être** as auxiliary in the passé composé: **est arrivé** (arrived — masculine singular). The so-called 'DR MRS VANDERTRAMP' verbs of movement and change of state take **être**: **arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, naître, mourir, rester, tomber, venir, aller** and all pronominal verbs. Past participle **arrivé** agrees with the subject Antoine (masculine singular) — no extra ending.
**A ressenti** — **ressentir** (to feel, to experience an emotion) in the passé composé: **a ressenti** (felt). **Ressentir** is used for felt emotions and physical sensations, while **sentir** can mean to smell or to feel (physically). **Un mélange de** = a mix of — **mélange** is masculine: **un mélange de joie et de tristesse** (a mix of joy and sadness). Note **d'une légère intimidation**: the partitive/indefinite article is maintained after **de** when an adjective precedes the noun in this construction — **d'une légère intimidation** rather than **de légère intimidation**.

sentences.base_language: A friendly personal trainer at the reception offered to show him around the equipment.
sentences.target_language: Un coach sportif sympathique à l'accueil lui a proposé de lui faire visiter les équipements.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a proposé de** — indirect object pronoun **lui** (to him) placed before the auxiliary: **lui a proposé** (offered him — lit. proposed to him). **Proposer à quelqu'un de faire** = to offer someone to do, to suggest to someone that they do: **proposer à Antoine de visiter** → **lui a proposé de visiter**. The indirect object pronoun **lui** replaces **à Antoine** and precedes the auxiliary in compound tenses.
**Lui faire visiter** — **faire + infinitif** causative construction meaning to show around, to have someone visit: **faire visiter les équipements** (to show around the equipment — lit. to have him visit the equipment). **Faire visiter** is the standard construction for showing someone around a place: **faire visiter la ville** (to show someone around the city), **faire visiter un appartement** (to show someone round a flat). The second **lui** is the indirect object of the infinitive **visiter**: **lui faire visiter** = to show him around. In **faire + infinitif**, object pronouns of the infinitive precede **faire**.

sentences.base_language: The trainer explained that consistency was more important than intensity, especially at the beginning.
sentences.target_language: Le coach lui a expliqué que la régularité était plus importante que l'intensité, surtout au début.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a expliqué que… était** — reported speech with tense concordance: the direct speech equivalent would be **la régularité est plus importante** (present tense). After a past verb of saying or explaining (**a expliqué**), the present shifts to the imparfait in indirect speech: **qu'elle était** (that it was). This **concordance des temps** is obligatory in formal French: **il a dit qu'il viendrait** (he said he would come — future → conditional), **elle a expliqué qu'il fallait** (she explained that it was necessary — present → imparfait).
**Plus importante que** — comparative of superiority with an adjective: **plus + adjective + que** = more … than. **Important** is a regular adjective; the feminine form **importante** agrees with **la régularité** (feminine). Note that **bon** and **mauvais** have irregular comparatives: **meilleur** (better) and **pire/plus mauvais** (worse). **Surtout au début** = especially at the beginning — **surtout** (especially, above all) is a common discourse adverb: **surtout en hiver** (especially in winter), **surtout ne pas** (above all, don't).

sentences.base_language: Antoine started with twenty minutes on the treadmill, which felt manageable at first.
sentences.target_language: Antoine a commencé par vingt minutes sur le tapis de course, ce qui lui a semblé gérable dans un premier temps.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A commencé par** — **commencer par + infinitif ou nom** = to start by, to start with: **a commencé par vingt minutes** (started with twenty minutes). **Commencer par** introduces the first item in a sequence: **commencez par lire les instructions** (start by reading the instructions), **il a commencé par se plaindre** (he started by complaining). Compare: **commencer à + infinitif** = to begin to do (emphasising the start of an action): **il a commencé à courir** (he started running). The preposition changes the meaning: **par** = sequence; **à** = onset of an ongoing action.
**Ce qui lui a semblé gérable** — **ce qui** as subject of a relative clause referring to the whole preceding idea: **ce qui** (which — referring to the twenty minutes on the treadmill as a whole concept, not a specific noun). **Ce qui** is used when the antecedent is a clause or idea rather than a specific noun: **il est parti tôt, ce qui m'a surpris** (he left early, which surprised me). **Sembler + adjectif** = to seem, to appear: **lui a semblé gérable** (seemed manageable to him). **Lui** is the indirect object (to him). **Dans un premier temps** = at first, initially — a more formal alternative to **au début** or **d'abord**.

sentences.base_language: By the tenth minute, his legs were heavy, his lungs were burning, and sweat was running down his face.
sentences.target_language: À la dixième minute, ses jambes étaient lourdes, ses poumons brûlaient et la sueur lui coulait le long du visage.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Étaient lourdes / brûlaient / lui coulait** — imparfait for multiple simultaneous ongoing states and sensations: all three verbs describe conditions that were in progress at the same moment (the tenth minute). The imparfait is the tense of description and background in past narratives — it paints what was happening at a given moment rather than advancing the plot. Contrast: **à ce moment-là, il a trébuché** (just then, he stumbled — passé composé for a sudden completed event).
**Lui coulait le long du visage** — **couler le long de** = to run/flow along, to trickle down: **la sueur lui coulait le long du visage** (sweat was running down his face). **Lui** is the indirect object (dative of reference/interest), indicating the person affected: **lui coulait** = was running down on him, was flowing along his face. This dative of interest (**datif éthique**) is common with body parts: **les jambes lui tremblaient** (his legs were trembling — lit. the legs were trembling on him), **le cœur lui battait fort** (his heart was pounding).

sentences.base_language: He slowed the machine to a walking pace, telling himself this still counted as progress.
sentences.target_language: Il a ralenti le tapis à l'allure de la marche, se disant que cela comptait quand même comme un progrès.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se disant que** — **se dire** (pronominal) used as a present participle in an appositive phrase expressing simultaneous inner speech: **se disant que** (telling himself that). **Se dire** = to say to oneself, to think to oneself — a reflective inner monologue: **il se disait qu'il faisait de son mieux** (he told himself he was doing his best). The present participle without **en** here indicates an action simultaneous with the main verb (**a ralenti**).
**Comptait quand même comme un progrès** — imparfait in reported thought after a past main clause verb: **se disant que cela comptait** (telling himself that this counted). The present tense of the direct thought (**ça compte comme un progrès**) shifts to the imparfait in reported thought after a past-tense context. **Compter comme** = to count as, to be considered as: **ça compte comme un effort** (it counts as an effort). **Quand même** = still, all the same, nevertheless — a very common French discourse particle: **c'est quand même bien** (it's still good, it's good all the same).

sentences.base_language: Next, the trainer showed him a series of strength exercises using free weights.
sentences.target_language: Ensuite, le coach lui a montré une série d'exercices de musculation avec des haltères.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a montré** — indirect object pronoun **lui** (to him/her) placed before the auxiliary: **lui a montré** (showed him — lit. showed to him). **Montrer quelque chose à quelqu'un** = to show something to someone: **montrer les exercices à Antoine** → **lui a montré les exercices**. The indirect object pronoun **lui** replaces **à Antoine** and is placed directly before the auxiliary **a** in the passé composé.
**Une série de + nom pluriel** — **une série de** = a series of, a set of, followed by a plural noun: **une série d'exercices** (a series of exercises). After **une série de**, **une dizaine de**, **une foule de**, the verb agrees with **une série** (singular subject), not the plural noun: **une série d'exercices a été présentée** (a series of exercises was presented). **Des haltères** (m. pl.) = dumbbells, free weights — **un haltère** is masculine despite the feminine-looking ending. **De musculation** = of strength training — **la musculation** = bodybuilding, strength/weight training: **salle de musculation** (weights room).

sentences.base_language: Antoine's arms trembled on the last two repetitions of each set, which the trainer said was a good sign.
sentences.target_language: Les bras d'Antoine tremblaient aux deux dernières répétitions de chaque série, ce que le coach a dit être un bon signe.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tremblaient** — imparfait for a repeated or habitual action occurring each time within the ongoing training session: **tremblaient aux deux dernières répétitions de chaque série** (trembled on the last two repetitions of each set). The imparfait is used here not for a unique event but for a pattern that recurred with each set. Compare: **les bras lui ont tremblé une fois** (his arms trembled once — passé composé for a single completed event).
**Ce que le coach a dit être** — **ce que** as direct object of the relative clause: **ce que le coach a dit** (what the trainer said). **Ce que** refers to the whole preceding idea (arms trembling) as the object of **dire**. **Dire + infinitif** in the construction **a dit être** (said to be / said it was): **le coach a dit [cela] être un bon signe** = the coach said this was a good sign. This infinitival construction after **dire** is formal; a more spoken alternative would be **ce que le coach a dit que c'était un bon signe**. **Un bon signe** = a good sign — **signe** is masculine.

sentences.base_language: After the session, every muscle in his body ached in a deep but not unpleasant way.
sentences.target_language: Après la séance, chaque muscle de son corps était douloureux d'une façon profonde mais pas désagréable.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Chaque muscle… était douloureux** — **chaque** (each, every) is always singular and requires a singular verb: **chaque muscle était** (every muscle was — singular). **Chaque** has no plural form: **chaque enfant** (every child), **chaque fois** (each time). Contrast: **tous les muscles** (all the muscles — plural with plural verb). **Être douloureux** = to be aching, to be painful — **douloureux/douloureuse** is the adjective from **la douleur** (pain): **une zone douloureuse** (a painful area), **une sensation douloureuse** (a painful sensation).
**D'une façon… mais pas** — **d'une façon + adjective** = in a … way: **d'une façon profonde** (in a deep way), **d'une façon agréable** (in a pleasant way). This construction is synonymous with **d'une manière + adjective**: **d'une manière douce** (gently, in a gentle way). **Pas désagréable** — the negative **ne… pas** with an adjective used predicatively: in this nominal phrase, **ne** is omitted (as is common in informal and even standard written French within embedded phrases): **d'une façon … pas désagréable** (in a not-unpleasant way). Full form: **qui n'était pas désagréable**.

sentences.base_language: He sat in the changing room for a few minutes, too tired to move but feeling genuinely proud.
sentences.target_language: Il s'est assis dans le vestiaire quelques minutes, trop fatigué pour bouger mais se sentant sincèrement fier.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est assis** — pronominal verb **s'asseoir** with **être** auxiliary in the passé composé: **s'est assis** (sat down — masculine singular). **S'asseoir** is highly irregular: past participle **assis**, present **je m'assieds** or **je m'assois**, imparfait **s'asseyait**. The reflexive pronoun **se** precedes the auxiliary. Past participle **assis** agrees with the subject Antoine (masculine singular) — no additional ending. **S'asseoir** (action: to sit down) vs **être assis** (state: to be sitting/seated).
**Trop fatigué pour bouger** — **trop + adjective + pour + infinitif** = too (adjective) to do: **trop fatigué pour bouger** (too tired to move). **Trop… pour** is the standard construction expressing excess preventing an action: **trop lent pour gagner** (too slow to win), **trop tôt pour partir** (too early to leave). **Se sentant sincèrement fier** — present participle of **se sentir** used appositively to express a simultaneous state: **se sentant fier** (feeling proud). **Se sentir + adjective** = to feel: **se sentir libre** (to feel free), **se sentir fier** (to feel proud). **Sincèrement** = genuinely, sincerely — placed before the adjective it modifies.

sentences.base_language: He drank a large bottle of water and ate a banana on a bench outside.
sentences.target_language: Il a bu une grande bouteille d'eau et mangé une banane sur un banc à l'extérieur.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A bu… et mangé** — when two past participles share the same auxiliary in a coordinated passé composé, the auxiliary is not repeated: **a bu… et mangé** (drank… and ate). The auxiliary **a** governs both **bu** (past participle of **boire** — irregular) and **mangé** (past participle of **manger** — regular). This ellipsis of the repeated auxiliary is standard: **il a chanté et dansé** (he sang and danced). **Boire** is irregular: present **boit**, passé composé **a bu**, past participle **bu**.
**Une grande bouteille d'eau** — the adjective **grande** precedes the noun, as **grand** belongs to the BAGS/BANGS group of adjectives (Beauty, Age, Number, Goodness, Size) that normally precede the noun: **un grand homme** (a great/tall man), **une grande salle** (a large room). **D'eau** — after a noun expressing a container or measure, **de** introduces the contents without an article: **une bouteille d'eau** (a bottle of water), **un verre de jus** (a glass of juice), **une tasse de café** (a cup of coffee). **À l'extérieur** = outside, on the outside — **à l'intérieur** (inside) is the antonym.

sentences.base_language: Walking home, his legs felt heavy but his mood had lifted enormously.
sentences.target_language: En rentrant chez lui, ses jambes lui semblaient lourdes mais son humeur s'était considérablement améliorée.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En rentrant** — **en + gérondif** (present participle) expressing simultaneity: **en rentrant chez lui** (while walking home, on his way home). The gérondif is formed with **en** + present participle (verb stem + **-ant**): **en marchant** (while walking), **en parlant** (while speaking). It always refers to the subject of the main clause. Compare **tout en rentrant** (which adds a nuance of continuity or mild concession) with **en rentrant** (neutral simultaneity or manner).
**S'était considérablement améliorée** — plus-que-parfait of a pronominal verb: **s'était améliorée** (had improved — feminine, agreeing with **son humeur**). The plus-que-parfait expresses an action completed before another past action or reference point: **en rentrant** (reference point: while walking home), **son humeur s'était améliorée** (his mood had [already] lifted). With pronominal verbs, the auxiliary is **être** in the imparfait (**s'était**) + past participle. Agreement: the past participle **améliorée** agrees with the subject **son humeur** (feminine singular) → **améliorée**. **Considérablement** = considerably, enormously — placed between the auxiliary and past participle in compound tenses.

sentences.base_language: That evening, his muscles began to stiffen and he had to lower himself very carefully into every chair.
sentences.target_language: Ce soir-là, ses muscles ont commencé à se raidir et il a dû s'asseoir très prudemment dans chaque chaise.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ont commencé à se raidir** — **commencer à + infinitif** = to begin to, to start to, marking the onset of an action: **ont commencé à se raidir** (began to stiffen). **Commencer à** is followed by an infinitive and signals the beginning of a new development — it advances the narrative and so takes the passé composé. Compare **se mettre à + infinitif** which implies a more sudden or resolute start: **il s'est mis à pleuvoir** (it started raining — suddenly). **Se raidir** = to stiffen, to tense up — pronominal verb: **se raidir de froid** (to stiffen with cold).
**A dû s'asseoir** — **devoir** in the passé composé expressing an obligation that was fulfilled: **a dû s'asseoir** (had to sit down — and did so). The passé composé of **devoir** (= **avoir** + **dû**) conveys a specific obligation that was acted upon: **j'ai dû partir** (I had to leave and did). Compare the imparfait **devait**: **il devait s'asseoir mais il restait debout** (he was supposed to sit but remained standing — obligation not necessarily fulfilled). **Très prudemment** = very carefully — **prudemment** is the adverb from **prudent** (careful), formed irregularly: adjective stem + **-emment** (as with adjectives ending in **-ent**): **prudemment, patiemment, différemment**.

sentences.base_language: Despite the soreness, Antoine felt a sense of determination he hadn't felt in years.
sentences.target_language: Malgré les courbatures, Antoine ressentait une détermination qu'il n'avait pas ressentie depuis des années.
sentences.grammarNote:
**N'avait pas ressentie depuis des années** — plus-que-parfait with **depuis** in a negative context: **n'avait pas ressentie depuis des années** (hadn't felt in years). In the negative, **depuis** + plus-que-parfait describes an action that had not occurred up to the reference point: **il n'avait pas dormi depuis deux jours** (he hadn't slept for two days). This contrasts with the positive imparfait + **depuis** (action ongoing): **il attendait depuis des années** (he had been waiting for years). Past participle agreement: **ressentie** agrees with the preceding direct object **qu'** (relative pronoun replacing **une détermination** — feminine singular) → **ressentie**.
**Ressentait** — imparfait for an emotional state felt at the time: **ressentait une détermination** (felt a sense of determination — an ongoing inner state during that evening). The imparfait is preferred for emotional and mental states in past narratives: **il ressentait de la fierté** (he felt pride), **elle savait que** (she knew that). **Malgré** = despite, in spite of — preposition followed directly by a noun (no verb): **malgré la fatigue** (despite the tiredness), **malgré tout** (despite everything). Not to be confused with **bien que** + subjunctive (although).

sentences.base_language: He downloaded a fitness app and set himself a goal of going to the gym three times a week.
sentences.target_language: Il a téléchargé une application de sport et s'est fixé comme objectif d'y aller trois fois par semaine.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est fixé comme objectif** — **se fixer un objectif** = to set oneself a goal: **s'est fixé comme objectif de** (set himself the goal of). **Fixer** = to fix, to set; **se fixer** (pronominal) = to set for oneself: **se fixer un but** (to set oneself a target), **se fixer des limites** (to set oneself limits). **Comme objectif** (as a goal) specifies the role of what is fixed. The past participle **fixé** does not agree with the preceding reflexive **se** here because **se** is the indirect object (the direct object is **comme objectif d'y aller**) — no agreement marker.
**D'y aller** — **y** replacing a place already mentioned (**à la salle de sport**): **d'y aller** (of going there). **Y** stands in for **à + place** and precedes the infinitive: **y aller** (to go there), **y retourner** (to go back there). The construction **s'est fixé comme objectif d'y aller** uses **de + infinitif** after **objectif**: **l'objectif de faire** (the goal of doing). **Trois fois par semaine** = three times a week — **par** expresses frequency in distributions: **deux fois par jour** (twice a day), **une fois par mois** (once a month).

sentences.base_language: The session two days later was noticeably easier — his body had already started to adapt.
sentences.target_language: La séance deux jours plus tard était nettement plus facile — son corps avait déjà commencé à s'adapter.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait déjà commencé à s'adapter** — plus-que-parfait expressing an action already completed before the reference point (the second session): **avait déjà commencé à s'adapter** (had already started to adapt). The plus-que-parfait (imparfait of **avoir** or **être** + past participle) situates an action as anterior to another past event. **Déjà** (already) reinforces the sense of prior completion and is typically placed between the auxiliary and the past participle in compound tenses: **avait déjà commencé**.
**Nettement plus facile** — comparative of superiority with an adverb of degree: **nettement** (noticeably, clearly) intensifies the comparative **plus facile** (easier). **Nettement** = clearly, distinctly, noticeably — a common intensifier before comparatives: **nettement mieux** (noticeably better), **nettement plus rapide** (considerably faster). **Était… plus facile** — imparfait for a descriptive quality of the session: the second session's relative ease is a background descriptive fact, not a completed event, so the imparfait is used rather than the passé composé.

sentences.base_language: The trainer greeted him by name and said his technique with the weights had already improved.
sentences.target_language: Le coach l'a salué par son prénom et lui a dit que sa technique avec les haltères s'était déjà améliorée.
sentences.grammarNote:
**L'a salué** — direct object pronoun **l'** (him) placed before the auxiliary: **l'a salué** (greeted him). **Saluer** = to greet, to say hello to — takes a direct object: **saluer quelqu'un** (to greet someone). The pronoun **le** (contracted to **l'** before the vowel of **a**) replaces **Antoine** as the direct object. Past participle **salué** does not agree here because the direct object pronoun **l'** comes before the verb but refers to a masculine singular antecedent — **salué** remains unchanged (masculine singular = base form).
**S'était déjà améliorée** — plus-que-parfait of a pronominal verb in reported speech: **que sa technique s'était déjà améliorée** (that his technique had already improved). After a past reporting verb (**a dit**), a past perfect equivalent in direct speech (**s'est améliorée** — passé composé) shifts to the plus-que-parfait in indirect speech: **il a dit que sa technique s'était améliorée**. Agreement: **améliorée** agrees with **sa technique** (feminine singular). **Par son prénom** = by (his) first name — **appeler quelqu'un par son prénom** (to call someone by their first name); **par** introduces the means or manner.

sentences.base_language: After a month of regular training, his energy levels and mood had changed in ways the people around him began to notice.
sentences.target_language: Après un mois d'entraînement régulier, son niveau d'énergie et son humeur avaient changé d'une façon que les gens autour de lui ont commencé à remarquer.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avaient changé** — plus-que-parfait expressing a state of change already achieved before the people around him began to notice: **avaient changé** (had changed). The plus-que-parfait establishes the transformation as anterior to the moment of noticing (**ont commencé à remarquer** — passé composé). **Changer** in the sense of to change (intransitively) takes **avoir** as auxiliary: **son humeur a changé** (his mood changed — passé composé), **avait changé** (had changed — plus-que-parfait). Note: **changer** takes **être** only when used pronominally (**se changer** = to change one's clothes).
**D'une façon que… ont commencé à remarquer** — relative clause with **que** as direct object of the relative: **la façon que les gens ont commencé à remarquer** (the way that the people around him began to notice). **Que** is the relative pronoun standing in for **d'une façon** as the direct object of **remarquer**. The passé composé **ont commencé à remarquer** advances the narrative — the noticing is a new event. **Finir par + infinitif** would also be natural here: **les gens ont fini par le remarquer** (the people around him ended up noticing it — with **finir par** emphasising that it happened eventually).

sentences.base_language: What had started as an obligation had quietly become something he genuinely looked forward to each week.
sentences.target_language: Ce qui avait commencé comme une obligation était devenu discrètement quelque chose qu'il attendait avec impatience chaque semaine.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ce qui avait commencé… était devenu** — two plus-que-parfaits used together: **avait commencé** (had started) and **était devenu** (had become), both establishing a completed transformation in the story's past. **Ce qui** = what, that which — **ce qui** is the subject form of the indefinite relative pronoun (**ce que** is the object form): **ce qui m'intéresse** (what interests me), **ce que je veux** (what I want). **Était devenu** — **devenir** (to become) takes **être** as auxiliary in compound tenses: **est devenu** (became — passé composé), **était devenu** (had become — plus-que-parfait). Agreement: **devenu** agrees with **ce qui** → **ce** is neuter/masculine singular → **devenu** (no additional ending).
**Attendait avec impatience** — **attendre quelque chose avec impatience** = to look forward to something (lit. to wait for something with impatience). This is the standard French equivalent of 'to look forward to': **j'attends les vacances avec impatience** (I'm looking forward to the holidays). **Chaque semaine** = each week, every week — **chaque** + singular noun expresses regular recurrence: **chaque jour** (every day), **chaque matin** (every morning). **Discrètement** = quietly, discreetly — placed after the auxiliary and before the past participle in compound tenses: **était discrètement devenu** would also be acceptable but **devenu discrètement** (post-participle placement) is used here for rhythm.
